title: Changelog
description: Release notes for Pyxle. While we're in beta (`0.x`), minor versions may include breaking changes — those are called out explicitly here. To upgrade, run `pip install --upgrade pyxle-framework`.
html:
Release notes for Pyxle. While we're in beta (0.x), minor versions may include breaking changes — those are called out explicitly here. To upgrade, run pip install --upgrade pyxle-framework.

0.6.0 — 2026-07-01

  • AI accessibility — serve your app as markdown, plus llms.txt. A new opt-in llms config block makes every Pyxle app legible to AI assistants and coding agents. Enable it ("llms": true) and the framework serves a clean markdown rendition of each page at its URL with .md appended (/docs/routing.md), returns markdown from the same URL to requests that send Accept: text/markdown (browsers are unaffected), serves an /llms.txt index, and advertises it with Link/X-Llms-Txt discovery headers. A page's markdown is resolved from your project — a co-located <page>.md file, a to_markdown handler in the page's server module, or a to_markdown in the nearest ancestor llms.py (which scopes to a whole route subtree; pages/llms.py is app-wide) — with an opt-in autoConvert HTML→markdown fallback and a redirect-to-the-page fallback when nothing resolves. /llms.txt comes from a static public/llms.txt, a llms_txt hook in pages/llms.py, or a generated index, and a wrap_markdown hook in pages/llms.py can frame every .md response with a header/footer (e.g. agent navigation hints). Off by default; adds nothing to the page hot path. See the AI accessibility guide and Configuration → AI accessibility.

0.5.0 — 2026-06-18

The depth release: caching/SSG/ISR, streaming SSR, realtime (WebSockets + a cross-worker Redis broker), Pydantic-validated actions, observability, background work, image optimization, and multi-worker serving — built, documented, and dogfooded on pyxle.dev. Still 0.x (beta); the two behavior changes below are called out explicitly.

  • Cross-worker realtime — a Redis pub/sub broker. WebSocket channels (pyxle.realtime.channel / room.publish) now span worker processes and machines. The default InProcessBroker lives in one process, so under pyxle serve --workers N a client on one worker never saw messages published on another. Set PYXLE_REALTIME_BROKER=redis (with the pyxle-framework[redis] extra and PYXLE_REALTIME_REDIS_URL) and every worker relays through Redis: a message published on any worker reaches every subscriber on every worker, delivered once per connection. No code change — your handler's channel(ws, "room") / room.publish(...) are identical; only the broker behind app.state.pyxle_broker changes. The connection is pinged at startup (a bad URL fails loudly) and the listener reconnects automatically. The in-process broker remains the default and is correct for pyxle dev and single-worker serve. See WebSockets → Cross-worker realtime with Redis.
  • Fix: streaming SSR now survives production gzip. Production serves enable gzip, and Starlette's stock GZipMiddleware buffers the whole streamed response (it never flushes the compressor between chunks), so a streamed <Suspense> page arrived all at once instead of shell-first — defeating streaming for every app, since browsers always send Accept-Encoding: gzip. Pyxle now ships a streaming-aware StreamingGZipMiddleware (from pyxle.middleware) that flushes the compressor after every chunk, so the shell's compressed bytes reach the browser immediately. Compression and streaming are both preserved under pyxle serve, no configuration needed. See Streaming SSR → Streaming survives gzip.
  • Fix: dynamic pages are no longer client-nav-cached with stale data. The client navigation cache previously held every route's loader payload for the default window (2 minutes), so navigating away from and back to a dynamic page (a @server loader with no declared cache lifetime — e.g. a live feed) showed stale data until a hard refresh. The navigation-cache TTL now mirrors the server's real cacheability: a dynamic page (rendered private, no-cache) is not nav-cached (TTL 0), so back/forward always refetches and a just-made mutation shows immediately. Pages opt into nav-caching via a cache entry; static, loader-less pages keep the 2-minute default. See Caching → Client navigation cache and Navigation cache TTL.
  • Fix: pyxle build --static pre-renders pages whose loaders use a plugin. Static pre-rendering ran page and layout loaders without an active plugin context, so any app that touched a plugin service in a loader — most commonly a layout loader calling pyxle-db's get_database() — crashed with No active plugin context and pre-rendered nothing (0 pages). The static builder now stands up the same plugin context a request sees (loads the configured plugins and runs their startup, opening DB connections) around the pre-render, then tears it down — so plugin-backed loaders run at build time exactly as they do at request time, which is how static generation is meant to work (read your data source at build). See Caching → Static pre-rendering.
  • Changed (behavior): stricter validation of nested config blocks. The cors, csrf, observability, and routeMiddleware blocks (and styling / the starlette & vite network blocks) now reject unknown keys at boot instead of silently ignoring them. A typo'd or mis-cased nested key — e.g. csrf.cookieSamesite instead of cookieSameSite — that booted before now fails loudly with a clear Unknown keys in '<block>' block error. This is a deliberate fail-loud fix: a silently-dropped key could leave a security-relevant default in place. Fix: correct the key name. See Configuration.
  • Fix: error.pyxl no longer leaks internal error details in production. When a loader or SSR render raised a non-author exception, the nearest error.pyxl boundary received the raw exception message (and the exception class name) as props — potentially a Node SSR stack trace or a filesystem path — which it could render to end users. In production the boundary now gets a generic "An unexpected error occurred." and a sanitized ServerError type; author-raised LoaderError / ActionError messages still pass through (they're meant to be shown). Dev keeps the real (secret-redacted) message. See Error Handling.
  • Fix: import.meta.env.PYXLE_PUBLIC_* is now substituted during SSR. The Vite define for public env vars was applied only to the client bundle, so at server render the expression was undefined — any public env var rendered into the initial HTML caused a hydration mismatch / blank first paint. The SSR esbuild step now gets the same define (same keys, same quoting), so server and client agree. See Environment variables → Client-side variables.
  • Fix: scaffold .gitignore no longer ignores .env. A fresh pyxle init git-ignored .env even though the env-vars doc tells you to commit .env / .env.development / .env.production and ignore only the .local variants. The scaffold now matches the doc.
  • Fix: <Image> emits a lowercase fetchpriority attribute. The priority image rendered camelCase fetchPriority, which React 18.3.1 rejects with an unknown-prop warning on every such image. It now emits the valid lowercase HTML attribute.
  • Fix: production gzip no longer prints I/O operation on closed file. StreamingGZipMiddleware now closes its GzipFile + buffer deterministically on every path (small/already-encoded responses, and streams interrupted mid-flight), so the GC no longer finalizes an open compressor and the noisy Exception ignored while finalizing file … I/O operation on closed file stderr messages are gone.
  • Fix: pyxle typecheck works out of the box on current TypeScript. The generated tsconfig.json used moduleResolution: "node10" and baseUrl, both deprecated in TypeScript 7.0, so tsc failed with TS5107/TS5101 on a new-enough TypeScript. It now uses "bundler" resolution (correct for the Vite/esbuild pipeline) with tsconfig-relative paths and no baseUrl.
  • Startup warning when a BaseHTTPMiddleware is paired with streaming routes. A custom middleware subclassing Starlette's BaseHTTPMiddleware buffers the whole response, which breaks streaming SSR (a <Suspense> page would arrive all at once / raise No response returned on a mid-stream disconnect). Pyxle now logs a clear warning at startup naming the offending middleware class and pointing to the pure-ASGI pattern. See Middleware.
  • Clear compile-time error for TypeScript syntax in a client block. A .pyxl client block is plain JSX — type annotations (const x: number), interface, generics, and as casts aren't supported there. Previously such syntax slipped past the compiler (Babel parses it) and failed later in esbuild/Vite with a confusing message pointing into a generated .pyxle-build path. The compiler now detects it up front and raises a clear error pointing at your .pyxl source line: "TypeScript syntax (…) isn't supported in a .pyxl client block yet — keep the client half plain JSX." Detection is AST-based (any TS-only node), so valid JSX — ternaries, object literals, the as prop — is never flagged. See TypeScript.
  • New guides: TypeScript and migrating from Flask/Django. TypeScript documents Pyxle's consumption-not-authoring model — the generated tsconfig.json and .d.ts declarations for every pyxle/client hook/component, the pyxle typecheck gate, and the honest limits (no .tsx authoring or per-page loader types yet). Migrating from Flask or Django maps the concepts you know (routes, views, templates, forms, the ORM, middleware, config) to their Pyxle equivalents, with a frank list of what has no direct equivalent (admin, url_for, flash messages, a core ORM).
  • error.pyxl is now a client-side error boundary too. The server already renders the nearest error.pyxl when a loader or the initial render fails; now that same boundary keeps working in the browser. If a component throws while re-rendering — after a state update, during a client-side navigation, or on a hydration fault — Pyxle catches it and renders the nearest error.pyxl in place, instead of React unmounting the page to a blank screen. The boundary receives the same error prop (message, statusCode, type) on both sides, so one error.pyxl renders identically wherever the fault happened. It is transparent until something throws (so hydration is byte-for-byte unchanged) and resets on the next navigation; the nearest error.pyxl is resolved per route (closest-ancestor walk-up) and threaded to the client like the streaming loading.pyxl boundary. In pyxle dev the error overlay still surfaces the full stack on top; in production the boundary is the user-facing fallback. This catches render faults — an error in an event handler or an await is handled where it occurs (a try/catch, or an ActionError from useAction). See Error Handling → Client-side errors.
  • Built-in rate limiting — pyxle.middleware.RateLimitMiddleware. A dependency-free token-bucket limiter, enabled from pyxle.config.json with no code: {"rateLimit": {"requests": 100, "window": 60}}. Each client (keyed by remote IP, or the first X-Forwarded-For hop when trustForwardedFor is set behind a trusted proxy) gets a bucket of requests tokens that refills at requests / window per second — so a brief burst is allowed while the sustained rate is bounded, friendlier than a hard fixed window. Over-limit requests get 429 Too Many Requests with a Retry-After header. exemptPaths skips health checks and metrics scrapes (segment-boundary matching, like csrf.exemptPaths). The limiter sits just inside request observability, so a throttled request still gets a correlation id and is counted, but is rejected before CSRF, static serving, or the handler do any work; the bucket store is bounded by an LRU cap so a flood of unique clients can't grow memory without bound. Multi-worker caveat: the store is in-memory and per-process, so under pyxle serve --workers N the effective global cap is N × requests — for one shared limit, rate-limit at your reverse proxy. Off by default (requests: 0). See Middleware → Rate limiting and Security → Rate limiting.
  • Route policies now apply to @action endpoints. Previously, a route hook registered under routeMiddleware wrapped page and API routes but not @action calls — so an auth or rate-limit policy could be silently bypassed by hitting the action endpoint directly. Actions now run through the same route-hook pipeline; register action policies under the new routeMiddleware.actions list. Because an action replies with JSON, an action hook should reject with a JSON 401/403 rather than an HTML redirect (which is why actions get their own list instead of inheriting the page's hooks). See Middleware → Route-level hooks.
  • pyxle serve --workers 0 auto-detects the core count. Multi-core serving (pyxle serve --workers N, each worker an independent process sharing one socket) already shipped; now --workers 0 sizes the worker count to the host's CPU cores, so you don't hard-code it per machine. The Deployment guide gains a consolidated per-worker state table (which features are per-process — page cache, WebSocket pub/sub, metrics, background tasks — and the shared-backend escape hatch for each), rolling-deploy / graceful-restart guidance (blue-green via /readyz, systemd, and the gunicorn-preload option), and the rationale for the share-nothing, per-worker-SSR-pool design.
  • Build optimization: responsive <Image>, preload hints, and --analyze. <Image> (from pyxle/client) is now an optimized image on par with Next.js's for everything that doesn't need a server-side optimizer: pass a loader (a CDN like Cloudinary/imgix, or a build plugin) and it emits a responsive srcset across a device-size ladder; it also gains fill mode, sizes, quality, objectFit, and priority → fetchpriority="high" for the LCP image — plus the existing blur placeholder, fallbackSrc, lazy-loading, and layout-shift prevention. Without a loader it stays a clean <img> (no fake srcset that re-downloads the full image — resizing needs a real backend, which stays opt-in). Separately, the SSR shell now injects <link rel="modulepreload"> for each page's entry and the chunks it imports (from the build manifest's import graph), so they download in parallel with HTML parsing; pyxle build --analyze prints a dependency-free JS/CSS bundle-size report (raw + gzip); and the generated Vite config carries an explicit build block (faster builds — Vite's slow gzip-size reporting is off since --analyze covers it). JS/CSS minification, tree-shaking, and code-splitting were already on via Vite's production defaults. See Build Optimization.
  • Background tasks & deferred work. Two ways to run work off the request's critical path. Inside an @action, request.state.background.add_task(fn, *args) (a Starlette BackgroundTasks) runs after the response is sent — ideal for "send the welcome email after signup"; returning {"background": [fn, *args]} is shorthand for a single such task. For fire-and-forget work from anywhere (loaders, actions, API routes), the new pyxle.tasks.enqueue(fn, *args) schedules it on a small in-process worker pool — coroutine functions are awaited, plain callables run in a thread, a failing task is logged (never takes a worker down), and the queue is bounded (TaskQueueFull under overload). The queue starts/stops with the app. Multi-worker caveat: it's per-process (like the in-process WebSocket broker), so for durability or cross-worker/exactly-once semantics, hand off to a real job queue — the guide documents Celery, ARQ, and Dramatiq integration. See Background Tasks.
  • OpenTelemetry tracing (opt-in). Emit OpenTelemetry spans for each request plus child spans for the SSR render, @server loaders, and @action calls, so a slow page surfaces as a drill-down trace. It's the heaviest integration, so it's a separate optional extra (pip install "pyxle-framework[observability-otel]") and fully off by default — with the extra absent, spans are a zero-cost no-op (one boolean check), and the exporter endpoint comes from the standard OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT env var. Sampling defaults to 5%. A base Pyxle install pulls in no OpenTelemetry packages; enabling observability.otel without the extra fails loudly at startup. See Observability → OpenTelemetry tracing.
  • pyxle dev --dashboard — a live terminal observability panel. Opt into a compact panel that refreshes every few seconds with request throughput and error rate, status-class breakdown, request/SSR-render/loader/action latency, and the page-cache hit ratio. It reads the same in-process metrics (so it's free on the request path), is dependency-free (no rich), and is dev-only — in production you'd scrape the metrics endpoint instead. See Observability → Dev dashboard.
  • Structured access logging. Opt into one structured log line per request — method, path, status, duration_ms, and the request's correlation id — via observability.accessLog, in "console" (human-readable) or "json" format. The correlation id is bound into a context variable for the request's duration, so your own log calls pick it up too. Works with no new dependency (a stdlib JSON/console formatter); the optional [observability] extra adds structlog for richer rendering. Off by default so it won't disrupt an existing log pipeline. See Observability → Structured logging.
  • Observability: request IDs, timing, metrics, and richer health probes. Every request now gets a correlation id — surfaced as the X-Request-Id response header and on request.state.request_id, readable from any @server loader or @action — and its duration is measured, along with SSR-render, loader, action, and page-cache (hit/stale/miss) metrics. The request-id and timing are on by default (a uuid4 and two perf_counter reads, no I/O, added at the outermost layer so even a CSRF rejection is tagged); an incoming X-Request-Id is not trusted unless you opt in (observability.trustIncomingRequestId), since echoing a client value into logs is a spoofing vector. Metrics are recorded into a bounded, allocation-cheap in-process registry and exposed — opt-in — at a Prometheus endpoint (observability.metricsEndpoint, optionally bearer-guarded); the page-cache hit ratio comes from the existing server cache. /readyz now runs dependency checks (today: the SSR worker pool has a live worker) and returns 503 until the server is genuinely ready, while /healthz stays a pure liveness probe. All configured under the new observability block ("observability": false disables request-id/timing); the helper from pyxle.observability import get_request_id reads the id anywhere. Multi-worker note: metrics are per-worker (each series carries a worker label) — aggregate at the scraper. Structured logging, a pyxle dev dashboard, and OpenTelemetry land next in Phase 2.7. See Observability.
  • Typed request validation for @action with Pydantic. An action can now declare a body parameter annotated with a Pydantic model — async def register(request, body: Signup) — and Pyxle parses, coerces, and validates the request body before the action runs, passing in a fully-typed instance. No more hand-written await request.json() + key-by-key checks. On failure the action is not called and the client gets a 422 with a top-level fields map (field path → messages, dotted for nested models and indexed for list items: address.zip, tags.0). Both useAction and <Form> surface it — result.fields / the reactive submit.fields, and <Form onError={(message, fields) => …}> — so you can render messages next to each input. For checks Pydantic can't express (uniqueness, cross-field rules) raise the new ValidationActionError, which is auto-imported alongside ActionError and produces the same 422 shape. Because the body models are real Pydantic models, pyxle openapi generates an OpenAPI 3.1 document of every action's request body straight from your code (--out openapi.json), derived from runtime introspection so it always matches what the dispatcher validates. Pydantic is an optional dependency — install the extra with pip install "pyxle-framework[pydantic]"; actions without a model parameter are unaffected, and an action that declares one without Pydantic installed fails with a clear error rather than silently skipping validation. The generated useAction/Form runtimes now also ship TypeScript declarations (.d.ts). See Server Actions → Validating request bodies.
  • Fix: a server module that fails to import is no longer cached as a broken empty module. If a page's compiled server module raised during its first import (e.g. a bad top-level import), the half-initialised module was left in sys.modules, so every later request for that page silently got an empty module instead of re-raising the real error. Failed imports are now evicted, so the error surfaces consistently on each request.
  • WebSockets — page handlers, a client hook, and pub/sub. A page .pyxl can now export a module-scope async def websocket(ws) and Pyxle serves a WebSocket route at the page's path, alongside its HTTP render — the same file handles both protocols, and dynamic segments (pages/chat/[room].pyxl) resolve into ws.path_params. Detection is by convention (the function name, not a decorator), matching how pages/api/** modules already expose a websocket callable. The new pyxle.realtime module ships a small pub/sub layer: async with channel(ws, "room") as room: await room.publish(msg) subscribes a connection to a named channel and broadcasts to everyone in it, with stale-connection pruning. Because Starlette runs HTTP middleware only for http scope, a WS handler gets no middleware — so pyxle.realtime also provides authenticate_websocket(ws) (resolve the pyxle-auth session from the handshake cookie) and origin_allowed(ws, origins) (the WS equivalent of CSRF). On the client, the generated useWebSocket(path, options?) hook gives { status, send, lastMessage, error } with JSON parsing and exponential-backoff reconnect, and never opens a socket during SSR. Multi-worker caveat (called out honestly): the default in-process broker lives in one process, so under pyxle serve --workers N a message published on one worker isn't seen by clients on another — Pyxle logs a warning when it detects this, and the Broker protocol lets a Redis-backed broker drop in. See WebSockets.
  • Streaming SSR for <Suspense> pages. A page that wraps a slow part in a React 18 <Suspense> boundary now streams: Pyxle flushes the shell (everything outside the boundary) immediately for a fast time-to-first-byte, then streams each boundary's content in as it resolves. It's opt-in and zero-config — the compiler detects <Suspense> and only those pages stream; every other page keeps the buffered render byte-for-byte. The page hydrates exactly as before (the browser hydrates the same component), so no app changes are needed beyond adding the boundary. Streaming applies to dynamic, per-request pages only: a publicly cacheable route ({"data", "revalidate"} envelope, CACHE directive, or edge cache entry) still renders buffered so it can be stored and ETag'd, and a streamed response is always private, no-cache. The document head is flushed before the component renders, so a streamed page uses its static HEAD (a runtime-rendered <Head> lands too late — buffered pages are unaffected). A render error before the first byte falls back to the nearest error.pyxl rather than emitting a partial document. Built on the Node worker's new multi-frame renderToPipeableStream transport. See Streaming SSR.
  • loading.pyxl route-level loading states. A loading.pyxl file declares a loading component that wraps the whole route in <Suspense fallback={<Loading/>}> — streamed as the shell on the server and applied identically on the client so hydration matches. The nearest loading.pyxl wins (directory walk-up, like error.pyxl), and it composes with a page's own inner <Suspense>. Honest caveat: because the @server loader runs before render, the fallback only appears when the render suspends (a child using React.lazy or a thrown promise; Pyxle ships React 18.3.1, which has no React.use) — a page whose data comes entirely from its loader never suspends and streams at once. See Streaming SSR → Route-level loading states.
  • Server-side page caching with incremental regeneration. A @server loader can now return a {"data": ..., "revalidate": N} envelope to cache its rendered HTML for N seconds — later requests are served from the cache without re-running the loader or the Node SSR render. A loader-less page can opt in the same way with a module-level CACHE = {"revalidate": N} directive. And pyxle build --static pre-renders every loader-less, non-dynamic page to HTML at build time and warms the cache from it on startup, so the first request after a deploy is a hit — no cold render. Once the window passes, the stale page is served instantly while a single background re-render refreshes it (incremental static regeneration — no thundering herd). Purge a route on demand from an action with await cache.invalidate("/path") (or cache.invalidate_all()). Routes already declared in the pyxle.config.json edge cache block are server-cached automatically under the same TTL. Cached responses carry a strong ETag (so conditional requests get a 304) and an x-pyxle-cache: HIT|STALE|MISS header. The cache is enabled for pyxle serve (off in pyxle dev) and stores to a bounded in-memory backend by default, or to local disk or a shared Redis via PYXLE_PAGE_CACHE_BACKEND (Redis needs pip install 'pyxle-framework[redis]'). Only cache pages that render no per-user data — a cached render is shared with every visitor. See Caching.

0.4.5

  • Signed cookies & tokens — sign_cookie / verify_cookie. New stdlib-only helpers (importable from pyxle) attach a tamper-proof HMAC-SHA256 signature to any string — a session id, a "remember me" token, a stateless unsubscribe or password-reset link — and verify it later in constant time. The secret comes from PYXLE_SECRET_KEY (or an explicit secret_key=), and a salt= namespaces signatures so a token minted for one purpose can't be replayed for another. Signing without a secret raises MissingSecretKeyError rather than returning an unprotected value — it fails closed. See Security → Signed cookies and tokens.
  • Fix: PYXLE_PUBLIC_* client env vars now work in pyxle dev and no longer break pyxle build. The generated Vite define emitted each value as a bare expression, so a non-identifier value (an API URL, a 0x… Turnstile key) was silently dropped in dev and crashed vite build with esbuild's "Invalid define value". Values are now emitted as quoted JS string literals, so import.meta.env.PYXLE_PUBLIC_* resolves correctly in both dev and production builds. See Environment variables → Client-side variables.
  • Fix: web fonts and other CSS url() assets no longer 404 in pyxle dev. Vite rewrites @font-face/url(...) references to root-relative /@fs/… paths, which the browser resolved against Pyxle's origin instead of Vite's and 404'd — so @fontsource .woff2 files silently failed to load in development (production builds were unaffected, as their assets are hashed and served by Pyxle). Pyxle now sets Vite's server.origin, so dev assets load from Vite directly.

0.4.4

  • Fix: cross-page hash links scroll to their anchor. Client-side navigation to /page#section changed the page but stayed pinned to the top (only the URL carried the hash); a full load scrolled correctly. The router now jumps to the top and then scrolls to the anchor as soon as the next page's DOM commits, matching native behaviour — including for unknown anchors, which stay at the top.

0.4.3

  • Security: hardened HEAD sanitisation (XSS). Dynamic HEAD values/callables that interpolate loader data into raw head strings — the documented dynamic-meta-tags recipe — could previously inject markup because attribute values weren't escaped (a " in a <meta content> broke out of the attribute). Head elements are now parsed and rebuilt from a strict tag allowlist with every attribute value HTML-escaped, on* handlers dropped, javascript:/vbscript:/data: URLs neutralised, <meta http-equiv="refresh"> and non-head tags rejected, and any markup injected after a breakout discarded. Inline <script>/<style> head content remains supported as trusted author code. Self-closing head tags now serialise canonically as />.
  • Security: CSRF exempt paths match on segment boundaries. csrf.exemptPaths previously used a bare string prefix, so exempting /api/webhooks also exempted an adjacently-named sibling like /api/webhooks-admin. Exemptions now match a path exactly or at a / boundary.
  • Security: oversized no-JS form POSTs fail loud. A form-encoded POST whose body exceeded the CSRF buffer cap (1 MB) was silently truncated and replayed to your handler. Such requests now return 413 and ask for the token via the X-CSRF-Token header instead of corrupting the payload.
  • Fix: <Script>/<Image> boolean attributes written as strings. defer="false", async="no", priority="0", lazy="false" and similar quoted-falsy forms were coerced to True (the opposite of the source). They now coerce correctly.
  • Fix: custom csrf.cookieName / csrf.headerName now reach the client runtime. Configuring either name made every action POST fail with 403 — the middleware set and validated the cookie under the configured name while useAction/<Form> kept reading a literal pyxle-csrf cookie and sending a literal x-csrf-token header. Non-default names are now injected into the document shell as window.__PYXLE_CSRF_COOKIE__ / window.__PYXLE_CSRF_HEADER__ and the client runtime resolves them with the defaults as fallback, in both dev and production builds.
  • Fix: no double loader run on hover-then-click navigation. When a <Link> was prefetched on hover/viewport and then clicked, the click raced a second request and ran the page's @server loader twice. The click now reuses the in-flight prefetch, and a navigation-sequence token discards a superseded prefetch so a slow one can't render a stale page over a newer click.
  • Multi-core serving: pyxle serve --workers N. Production serving can now run N independent server processes on one port (each with its own SSR pool and event loop) — throughput scales with cores, no load balancer or shared state to configure. See Deployment → Multi-core (worker processes).
  • Sync API endpoints. A plain def endpoint(request) in pages/api/ now runs in Starlette's threadpool instead of failing at request time — blocking database drivers and sync SDKs no longer require manual asyncio.to_thread wrapping. Sync get/post/… methods on HTTPEndpoint classes are threadpooled too, and HTTPEndpoint classes dispatch correctly alongside route hooks. See API Routes → Sync endpoints and blocking calls.
  • In-memory static asset cache. When serving a production build, small static files (≤1 MB each, 32 MB total per process) are loaded into memory at startup and served without filesystem I/O or threadpool hops — several-fold higher static throughput. Conditional requests (ETag/If-Modified-Since → 304) and cache headers behave exactly as before; larger files keep streaming from disk.

0.4.2

  • Live dev-server reconciliation. Editing a .pyxl in pyxle dev now applies route-shape changes without a restart — rename/add/remove a @server loader or @action, add or delete a page, wrap a page in a layout, change the head. The route table is rebuilt and hot-swapped on every change (these previously needed rm -rf .pyxle-build + a restart). Editing pyxle.config.json prints a clear "restart to apply" warning instead of being silently ignored.
  • pyxle check works on a clean install. The JSX checker's parser dependencies are bundled into a self-contained extractor (shipped via pyxle-langkit), so pyxle check runs after pip install 'pyxle-framework[langkit]' with no extra npm setup. A missing-dependency failure now names the exact packages instead of an opaque error.
  • Locale-independent SSR. The Python↔Node SSR transport pins UTF-8, so emoji and other non-BMP characters in components or loader data no longer crash rendering under a non-UTF-8 locale (e.g. LANG=C).
  • Smoother first run. pyxle init generates a gitignored .env.local with a random dev PYXLE_SECRET_KEY (CSRF HMAC on out of the box); the scaffold requirements.txt now declares pyxle-framework itself; and pyxle install warns about PEP 668 externally-managed environments with venv guidance instead of letting pip throw a raw wall.
  • Docs: documented calling an @action endpoint directly for scripts and tests.

0.4.1

  • No more double loader run on first load. The page you land on is now seeded into the client navigation cache from the server render, so the active nav link's prefetch (a <Link> pointing at the current route) resolves from cache instead of re-fetching. The loader runs once, not twice — halving origin work on cold loads and stopping non-idempotent loaders (view counters, analytics) from firing twice. Back/forward navigation to the page is instant for the same reason.
  • Per-route navigation-cache TTL. A route's cache TTL now also governs how long its prefetched/seeded data stays fresh in the client navigation cache, so client freshness matches how long a CDN would serve the page. Routes without a cache entry use a default of 2 minutes (raised from 30s), tunable via the new navigation.defaultPrefetchTtl option.

0.4.0

  • Edge caching. Declare cacheable routes in pyxle.config.json::cache and pages are served Cache-Control: public, s-maxage=N (plus stale-while-revalidate) so a CDN or reverse proxy can absorb traffic instead of your origin — Pyxle's config-driven take on per-route revalidation. The per-user CSRF cookie is automatically omitted from cacheable responses. See Deployment → CDN and edge caching.
  • Hardened production errors. SSR render failures — including the SPA-navigation JSON path — are sanitized in the response (no exception type, message, or path leaks to the client) while the full error is written to the server log for operators.
  • Faster static serving. The static-asset middleware indexes public/client paths up front, skipping a per-request filesystem stat + exception on every dynamic request.
  • Layout & template loaders. A layout.pyxl or template.pyxl can declare its own @server loader; its result lands on the component's data prop, just like a page — so shared UI (nav bars, the signed-in user, the framework version) loads once per request without repeating the loader in every page. See Layouts → Layout data loaders.

0.3.0

  • First-class plugin system. Compose apps via pyxle.config.json::plugins (Django-style INSTALLED_APPS) — see the Plugins guide and Plugins API reference.
  • Django-style service access. Resolve any plugin-registered service with from pyxle.plugins import plugin and plugin("auth.service"), or use a typed shortcut shipped by the plugin (e.g. from pyxle_auth import get_auth_service).
  • First-party plugins. Two official plugins land: pyxle-db (SQLite-first with migrations) and pyxle-auth (email+password sessions, argon2id, rate limits).
  • WebSocket endpoints — pages/api/*.py can export async def websocket(ws) for live updates, chat, log streaming. See the API Routes guide.
  • Client navigation cache with TTL + invalidation. Loader payloads are cached for 30s by default (tunable) so back/forward navigation is instant. Call invalidate(url) from the client or return invalidate_routes(response, ...) from an @action to keep list views fresh after mutations — automatically honoured by useAction and <Form>.
  • ActionError is auto-imported for any .pyxl with an @action. No more NameError: name 'ActionError' is not defined on first try.
  • <Head> coerces multi-part <title> children into a single string, silencing React's "title element received an array" warning for the common <title>{name} — Brand</title> pattern.
  • SSR worker pins LANG=en-US.UTF-8 by default (override with PYXLE_SSR_LOCALE) so toLocaleString() and other Intl calls stop causing hydration mismatches.
  • Vite resolver prefers pinned versions. pyxle build now runs npm install before falling back to npx --yes vite, so builds honour your package.json pin instead of fetching vite@latest.
markdown:
# Changelog Release notes for Pyxle. While we're in beta (`0.x`), minor versions may include breaking changes — those are called out explicitly here. To upgrade, run `pip install --upgrade pyxle-framework`. ## 0.6.0 — 2026-07-01 - **AI accessibility — serve your app as markdown, plus `llms.txt`.** A new opt-in `llms` config block makes every Pyxle app legible to AI assistants and coding agents. Enable it (`"llms": true`) and the framework serves a clean **markdown** rendition of each page at its URL with `.md` appended (`/docs/routing.md`), returns markdown from the same URL to requests that send `Accept: text/markdown` (browsers are unaffected), serves an **`/llms.txt`** index, and advertises it with `Link`/`X-Llms-Txt` discovery headers. A page's markdown is resolved from your project — a co-located `.md` file, a `to_markdown` handler in the page's server module, or a `to_markdown` in the nearest ancestor `llms.py` (which scopes to a whole route subtree; `pages/llms.py` is app-wide) — with an opt-in `autoConvert` HTML→markdown fallback and a redirect-to-the-page fallback when nothing resolves. `/llms.txt` comes from a static `public/llms.txt`, a `llms_txt` hook in `pages/llms.py`, or a generated index, and a `wrap_markdown` hook in `pages/llms.py` can frame every `.md` response with a header/footer (e.g. agent navigation hints). Off by default; adds nothing to the page hot path. See the [AI accessibility guide](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/llms.md) and [Configuration → AI accessibility](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/configuration.md#ai-accessibility). ## 0.5.0 — 2026-06-18 The depth release: caching/SSG/ISR, streaming SSR, realtime (WebSockets + a cross-worker Redis broker), Pydantic-validated actions, observability, background work, image optimization, and multi-worker serving — built, documented, and dogfooded on pyxle.dev. Still `0.x` (beta); the two behavior changes below are called out explicitly. - **Cross-worker realtime — a Redis pub/sub broker.** WebSocket channels (`pyxle.realtime.channel` / `room.publish`) now span worker processes and machines. The default `InProcessBroker` lives in one process, so under `pyxle serve --workers N` a client on one worker never saw messages published on another. Set `PYXLE_REALTIME_BROKER=redis` (with the `pyxle-framework[redis]` extra and `PYXLE_REALTIME_REDIS_URL`) and every worker relays through Redis: a message published on any worker reaches every subscriber on every worker, delivered once per connection. **No code change** — your handler's `channel(ws, "room")` / `room.publish(...)` are identical; only the broker behind `app.state.pyxle_broker` changes. The connection is pinged at startup (a bad URL fails loudly) and the listener reconnects automatically. The in-process broker remains the default and is correct for `pyxle dev` and single-worker serve. See [WebSockets → Cross-worker realtime with Redis](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/websockets.md#cross-worker-realtime-with-redis). - **Fix: streaming SSR now survives production gzip.** Production serves enable gzip, and Starlette's stock `GZipMiddleware` buffers the whole streamed response (it never flushes the compressor between chunks), so a streamed `` page arrived all at once instead of shell-first — defeating streaming for every app, since browsers always send `Accept-Encoding: gzip`. Pyxle now ships a streaming-aware `StreamingGZipMiddleware` (from `pyxle.middleware`) that flushes the compressor after every chunk, so the shell's compressed bytes reach the browser immediately. Compression and streaming are both preserved under `pyxle serve`, no configuration needed. See [Streaming SSR → Streaming survives gzip](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/streaming.md#streaming-survives-gzip-in-production). - **Fix: dynamic pages are no longer client-nav-cached with stale data.** The client navigation cache previously held every route's loader payload for the default window (2 minutes), so navigating away from and back to a **dynamic** page (a `@server` loader with no declared cache lifetime — e.g. a live feed) showed stale data until a hard refresh. The navigation-cache TTL now mirrors the server's real cacheability: a dynamic page (rendered `private, no-cache`) is **not** nav-cached (TTL `0`), so back/forward always refetches and a just-made mutation shows immediately. Pages opt into nav-caching via a `cache` entry; static, loader-less pages keep the 2-minute default. See [Caching → Client navigation cache](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/caching.md#client-navigation-cache) and [Navigation cache TTL](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/client-api.md#navigation-cache-ttl). - **Fix: `pyxle build --static` pre-renders pages whose loaders use a plugin.** Static pre-rendering ran page and layout loaders without an active plugin context, so any app that touched a plugin service in a loader — most commonly a layout loader calling `pyxle-db`'s `get_database()` — crashed with `No active plugin context` and pre-rendered **nothing** (0 pages). The static builder now stands up the same plugin context a request sees (loads the configured plugins and runs their startup, opening DB connections) around the pre-render, then tears it down — so plugin-backed loaders run at build time exactly as they do at request time, which is how static generation is meant to work (read your data source at build). See [Caching → Static pre-rendering](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/caching.md#static-pre-rendering-pyxle-build-static). - **Changed (behavior): stricter validation of nested config blocks.** The `cors`, `csrf`, `observability`, and `routeMiddleware` blocks (and `styling` / the `starlette` & `vite` network blocks) now **reject unknown keys at boot** instead of silently ignoring them. A typo'd or mis-cased nested key — e.g. `csrf.cookieSamesite` instead of `cookieSameSite` — that booted before now fails loudly with a clear `Unknown keys in '' block` error. This is a deliberate fail-loud fix: a silently-dropped key could leave a security-relevant default in place. Fix: correct the key name. See [Configuration](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/configuration.md). - **Fix: `error.pyxl` no longer leaks internal error details in production.** When a loader or SSR render raised a non-author exception, the nearest `error.pyxl` boundary received the raw exception message (and the exception class name) as props — potentially a Node SSR stack trace or a filesystem path — which it could render to end users. In production the boundary now gets a generic `"An unexpected error occurred."` and a sanitized `ServerError` type; author-raised `LoaderError` / `ActionError` messages still pass through (they're meant to be shown). Dev keeps the real (secret-redacted) message. See [Error Handling](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/error-handling.md). - **Fix: `import.meta.env.PYXLE_PUBLIC_*` is now substituted during SSR.** The Vite `define` for public env vars was applied only to the client bundle, so at server render the expression was `undefined` — any public env var rendered into the initial HTML caused a hydration mismatch / blank first paint. The SSR esbuild step now gets the same `define` (same keys, same quoting), so server and client agree. See [Environment variables → Client-side variables](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/environment-variables.md). - **Fix: scaffold `.gitignore` no longer ignores `.env`.** A fresh `pyxle init` git-ignored `.env` even though the env-vars doc tells you to commit `.env` / `.env.development` / `.env.production` and ignore only the `.local` variants. The scaffold now matches the doc. - **Fix: `` emits a lowercase `fetchpriority` attribute.** The `priority` image rendered camelCase `fetchPriority`, which React 18.3.1 rejects with an unknown-prop warning on every such image. It now emits the valid lowercase HTML attribute. - **Fix: production gzip no longer prints `I/O operation on closed file`.** `StreamingGZipMiddleware` now closes its `GzipFile` + buffer deterministically on every path (small/already-encoded responses, and streams interrupted mid-flight), so the GC no longer finalizes an open compressor and the noisy `Exception ignored while finalizing file … I/O operation on closed file` stderr messages are gone. - **Fix: `pyxle typecheck` works out of the box on current TypeScript.** The generated `tsconfig.json` used `moduleResolution: "node10"` and `baseUrl`, both deprecated in TypeScript 7.0, so `tsc` failed with TS5107/TS5101 on a new-enough TypeScript. It now uses `"bundler"` resolution (correct for the Vite/esbuild pipeline) with tsconfig-relative `paths` and no `baseUrl`. - **Startup warning when a `BaseHTTPMiddleware` is paired with streaming routes.** A custom middleware subclassing Starlette's `BaseHTTPMiddleware` buffers the whole response, which breaks streaming SSR (a `` page would arrive all at once / raise `No response returned` on a mid-stream disconnect). Pyxle now logs a clear warning at startup naming the offending middleware class and pointing to the pure-ASGI pattern. See [Middleware](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/middleware.md). - **Clear compile-time error for TypeScript syntax in a client block.** A `.pyxl` client block is plain JSX — type annotations (`const x: number`), `interface`, generics, and `as` casts aren't supported there. Previously such syntax slipped past the compiler (Babel parses it) and failed later in esbuild/Vite with a confusing message pointing into a generated `.pyxle-build` path. The compiler now detects it up front and raises a clear error pointing at your `.pyxl` source line: "TypeScript syntax (…) isn't supported in a .pyxl client block yet — keep the client half plain JSX." Detection is AST-based (any TS-only node), so valid JSX — ternaries, object literals, the `as` prop — is never flagged. See [TypeScript](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/typescript.md). - **New guides: TypeScript and migrating from Flask/Django.** [TypeScript](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/typescript.md) documents Pyxle's consumption-not-authoring model — the generated `tsconfig.json` and `.d.ts` declarations for every `pyxle/client` hook/component, the `pyxle typecheck` gate, and the honest limits (no `.tsx` authoring or per-page loader types yet). [Migrating from Flask or Django](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/migration-from-flask-django.md) maps the concepts you know (routes, views, templates, forms, the ORM, middleware, config) to their Pyxle equivalents, with a frank list of what has no direct equivalent (admin, `url_for`, flash messages, a core ORM). - **`error.pyxl` is now a client-side error boundary too.** The server already renders the nearest `error.pyxl` when a loader or the initial render fails; now that same boundary keeps working in the browser. If a component throws while re-rendering — after a state update, during a client-side navigation, or on a hydration fault — Pyxle catches it and renders the nearest `error.pyxl` in place, instead of React unmounting the page to a blank screen. The boundary receives the **same** `error` prop (`message`, `statusCode`, `type`) on both sides, so one `error.pyxl` renders identically wherever the fault happened. It is transparent until something throws (so hydration is byte-for-byte unchanged) and resets on the next navigation; the nearest `error.pyxl` is resolved per route (closest-ancestor walk-up) and threaded to the client like the streaming `loading.pyxl` boundary. In `pyxle dev` the error overlay still surfaces the full stack on top; in production the boundary is the user-facing fallback. This catches *render* faults — an error in an event handler or an `await` is handled where it occurs (a `try/catch`, or an `ActionError` from `useAction`). See [Error Handling → Client-side errors](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/error-handling.md#client-side-errors). - **Built-in rate limiting — `pyxle.middleware.RateLimitMiddleware`.** A dependency-free **token-bucket** limiter, enabled from `pyxle.config.json` with no code: `{"rateLimit": {"requests": 100, "window": 60}}`. Each client (keyed by remote IP, or the first `X-Forwarded-For` hop when `trustForwardedFor` is set behind a trusted proxy) gets a bucket of `requests` tokens that refills at `requests / window` per second — so a brief burst is allowed while the sustained rate is bounded, friendlier than a hard fixed window. Over-limit requests get `429 Too Many Requests` with a `Retry-After` header. `exemptPaths` skips health checks and metrics scrapes (segment-boundary matching, like `csrf.exemptPaths`). The limiter sits just inside request observability, so a throttled request still gets a correlation id and is counted, but is rejected before CSRF, static serving, or the handler do any work; the bucket store is bounded by an LRU cap so a flood of unique clients can't grow memory without bound. **Multi-worker caveat:** the store is in-memory and per-process, so under `pyxle serve --workers N` the effective global cap is `N × requests` — for one shared limit, rate-limit at your reverse proxy. Off by default (`requests: 0`). See [Middleware → Rate limiting](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/middleware.md#rate-limiting) and [Security → Rate limiting](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/security.md#rate-limiting). - **Route policies now apply to `@action` endpoints.** Previously, a route hook registered under `routeMiddleware` wrapped page and API routes but **not** `@action` calls — so an auth or rate-limit policy could be silently bypassed by hitting the action endpoint directly. Actions now run through the same route-hook pipeline; register action policies under the new `routeMiddleware.actions` list. Because an action replies with JSON, an action hook should reject with a JSON `401`/`403` rather than an HTML redirect (which is why actions get their own list instead of inheriting the page's hooks). See [Middleware → Route-level hooks](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/middleware.md#route-level-hooks). - **`pyxle serve --workers 0` auto-detects the core count.** Multi-core serving (`pyxle serve --workers N`, each worker an independent process sharing one socket) already shipped; now `--workers 0` sizes the worker count to the host's CPU cores, so you don't hard-code it per machine. The [Deployment guide](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/deployment.md#multi-core-worker-processes) gains a consolidated **per-worker state** table (which features are per-process — page cache, WebSocket pub/sub, metrics, background tasks — and the shared-backend escape hatch for each), **rolling-deploy / graceful-restart** guidance (blue-green via `/readyz`, systemd, and the gunicorn-preload option), and the rationale for the share-nothing, per-worker-SSR-pool design. - **Build optimization: responsive ``, preload hints, and `--analyze`.** `` (from `pyxle/client`) is now an optimized image on par with Next.js's for everything that doesn't need a server-side optimizer: pass a `loader` (a CDN like Cloudinary/imgix, or a build plugin) and it emits a responsive `srcset` across a device-size ladder; it also gains `fill` mode, `sizes`, `quality`, `objectFit`, and `priority` → `fetchpriority="high"` for the LCP image — plus the existing blur placeholder, `fallbackSrc`, lazy-loading, and layout-shift prevention. Without a loader it stays a clean `` (no fake srcset that re-downloads the full image — resizing needs a real backend, which stays opt-in). Separately, the SSR shell now injects `` for each page's entry and the chunks it imports (from the build manifest's import graph), so they download in parallel with HTML parsing; `pyxle build --analyze` prints a dependency-free JS/CSS bundle-size report (raw + gzip); and the generated Vite config carries an explicit `build` block (faster builds — Vite's slow gzip-size reporting is off since `--analyze` covers it). JS/CSS minification, tree-shaking, and code-splitting were already on via Vite's production defaults. See [Build Optimization](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/build-optimization.md). - **Background tasks & deferred work.** Two ways to run work off the request's critical path. Inside an `@action`, `request.state.background.add_task(fn, *args)` (a Starlette `BackgroundTasks`) runs after the response is sent — ideal for "send the welcome email after signup"; returning `{"background": [fn, *args]}` is shorthand for a single such task. For fire-and-forget work from anywhere (loaders, actions, API routes), the new `pyxle.tasks.enqueue(fn, *args)` schedules it on a small **in-process** worker pool — coroutine functions are awaited, plain callables run in a thread, a failing task is logged (never takes a worker down), and the queue is bounded (`TaskQueueFull` under overload). The queue starts/stops with the app. **Multi-worker caveat:** it's per-process (like the in-process WebSocket broker), so for durability or cross-worker/exactly-once semantics, hand off to a real job queue — the guide documents Celery, ARQ, and Dramatiq integration. See [Background Tasks](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/background-tasks.md). - **OpenTelemetry tracing (opt-in).** Emit OpenTelemetry spans for each request plus child spans for the SSR render, `@server` loaders, and `@action` calls, so a slow page surfaces as a drill-down trace. It's the heaviest integration, so it's a **separate** optional extra (`pip install "pyxle-framework[observability-otel]"`) and **fully off** by default — with the extra absent, spans are a zero-cost no-op (one boolean check), and the exporter endpoint comes from the standard `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT` env var. Sampling defaults to 5%. A base Pyxle install pulls in no OpenTelemetry packages; enabling `observability.otel` without the extra fails loudly at startup. See [Observability → OpenTelemetry tracing](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/observability.md#opentelemetry-tracing). - **`pyxle dev --dashboard` — a live terminal observability panel.** Opt into a compact panel that refreshes every few seconds with request throughput and error rate, status-class breakdown, request/SSR-render/loader/action latency, and the page-cache hit ratio. It reads the same in-process metrics (so it's free on the request path), is **dependency-free** (no `rich`), and is dev-only — in production you'd scrape the metrics endpoint instead. See [Observability → Dev dashboard](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/observability.md#dev-dashboard). - **Structured access logging.** Opt into one structured log line per request — `method`, `path`, `status`, `duration_ms`, and the request's correlation id — via `observability.accessLog`, in `"console"` (human-readable) or `"json"` format. The correlation id is bound into a context variable for the request's duration, so your own log calls pick it up too. Works with **no new dependency** (a stdlib JSON/console formatter); the optional `[observability]` extra adds [structlog](https://www.structlog.org/) for richer rendering. Off by default so it won't disrupt an existing log pipeline. See [Observability → Structured logging](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/observability.md#structured-logging). - **Observability: request IDs, timing, metrics, and richer health probes.** Every request now gets a correlation id — surfaced as the `X-Request-Id` response header and on `request.state.request_id`, readable from any `@server` loader or `@action` — and its duration is measured, along with SSR-render, loader, action, and page-cache (hit/stale/miss) metrics. The request-id and timing are **on by default** (a `uuid4` and two `perf_counter` reads, no I/O, added at the outermost layer so even a CSRF rejection is tagged); an incoming `X-Request-Id` is **not** trusted unless you opt in (`observability.trustIncomingRequestId`), since echoing a client value into logs is a spoofing vector. Metrics are recorded into a bounded, allocation-cheap in-process registry and exposed — **opt-in** — at a Prometheus endpoint (`observability.metricsEndpoint`, optionally bearer-guarded); the page-cache hit ratio comes from the existing server cache. `/readyz` now runs **dependency checks** (today: the SSR worker pool has a live worker) and returns `503` until the server is genuinely ready, while `/healthz` stays a pure liveness probe. All configured under the new [`observability`](https://pyxle.dev/docs/reference/configuration.md#observability) block (`"observability": false` disables request-id/timing); the helper `from pyxle.observability import get_request_id` reads the id anywhere. **Multi-worker note:** metrics are per-worker (each series carries a `worker` label) — aggregate at the scraper. Structured logging, a `pyxle dev` dashboard, and OpenTelemetry land next in Phase 2.7. See [Observability](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/observability.md). - **Typed request validation for `@action` with Pydantic.** An action can now declare a body parameter annotated with a [Pydantic](https://docs.pydantic.dev/) model — `async def register(request, body: Signup)` — and Pyxle parses, coerces, and validates the request body before the action runs, passing in a fully-typed instance. No more hand-written `await request.json()` + key-by-key checks. On failure the action is **not** called and the client gets a `422` with a top-level `fields` map (field path → messages, dotted for nested models and indexed for list items: `address.zip`, `tags.0`). Both `useAction` and `
` surface it — `result.fields` / the reactive `submit.fields`, and ` …}>` — so you can render messages next to each input. For checks Pydantic can't express (uniqueness, cross-field rules) raise the new `ValidationActionError`, which is auto-imported alongside `ActionError` and produces the same `422` shape. Because the body models are real Pydantic models, `pyxle openapi` generates an [OpenAPI 3.1](https://spec.openapis.org/oas/v3.1.0) document of every action's request body straight from your code (`--out openapi.json`), derived from runtime introspection so it always matches what the dispatcher validates. Pydantic is an **optional dependency** — install the extra with `pip install "pyxle-framework[pydantic]"`; actions without a model parameter are unaffected, and an action that declares one without Pydantic installed fails with a clear error rather than silently skipping validation. The generated `useAction`/`Form` runtimes now also ship TypeScript declarations (`.d.ts`). See [Server Actions → Validating request bodies](https://pyxle.dev/docs/core-concepts/server-actions.md#validating-request-bodies-with-pydantic). - **Fix: a server module that fails to import is no longer cached as a broken empty module.** If a page's compiled server module raised during its first import (e.g. a bad top-level `import`), the half-initialised module was left in `sys.modules`, so every later request for that page silently got an *empty* module instead of re-raising the real error. Failed imports are now evicted, so the error surfaces consistently on each request. - **WebSockets — page handlers, a client hook, and pub/sub.** A page `.pyxl` can now export a module-scope `async def websocket(ws)` and Pyxle serves a WebSocket route at the page's path, alongside its HTTP render — the same file handles both protocols, and dynamic segments (`pages/chat/[room].pyxl`) resolve into `ws.path_params`. Detection is by convention (the function name, not a decorator), matching how `pages/api/**` modules already expose a `websocket` callable. The new `pyxle.realtime` module ships a small pub/sub layer: `async with channel(ws, "room") as room: await room.publish(msg)` subscribes a connection to a named channel and broadcasts to everyone in it, with stale-connection pruning. Because Starlette runs HTTP middleware only for `http` scope, a WS handler gets **no** middleware — so `pyxle.realtime` also provides `authenticate_websocket(ws)` (resolve the pyxle-auth session from the handshake cookie) and `origin_allowed(ws, origins)` (the WS equivalent of CSRF). On the client, the generated `useWebSocket(path, options?)` hook gives `{ status, send, lastMessage, error }` with JSON parsing and exponential-backoff reconnect, and never opens a socket during SSR. **Multi-worker caveat (called out honestly):** the default in-process broker lives in one process, so under `pyxle serve --workers N` a message published on one worker isn't seen by clients on another — Pyxle logs a warning when it detects this, and the `Broker` protocol lets a Redis-backed broker drop in. See [WebSockets](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/websockets.md). - **Streaming SSR for `` pages.** A page that wraps a slow part in a React 18 `` boundary now streams: Pyxle flushes the shell (everything outside the boundary) immediately for a fast time-to-first-byte, then streams each boundary's content in as it resolves. It's opt-in and zero-config — the compiler detects `` and only those pages stream; every other page keeps the buffered render byte-for-byte. The page hydrates exactly as before (the browser hydrates the same component), so no app changes are needed beyond adding the boundary. Streaming applies to dynamic, per-request pages only: a publicly cacheable route (`{"data", "revalidate"}` envelope, `CACHE` directive, or edge `cache` entry) still renders buffered so it can be stored and `ETag`'d, and a streamed response is always `private, no-cache`. The document head is flushed before the component renders, so a streamed page uses its static `HEAD` (a runtime-rendered `` lands too late — buffered pages are unaffected). A render error before the first byte falls back to the nearest `error.pyxl` rather than emitting a partial document. Built on the Node worker's new multi-frame `renderToPipeableStream` transport. See [Streaming SSR](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/streaming.md). - **`loading.pyxl` route-level loading states.** A `loading.pyxl` file declares a loading component that wraps the whole route in `}>` — streamed as the shell on the server and applied identically on the client so hydration matches. The nearest `loading.pyxl` wins (directory walk-up, like `error.pyxl`), and it composes with a page's own inner ``. Honest caveat: because the `@server` loader runs before render, the fallback only appears when the *render* suspends (a child using `React.lazy` or a thrown promise; Pyxle ships React 18.3.1, which has no `React.use`) — a page whose data comes entirely from its loader never suspends and streams at once. See [Streaming SSR → Route-level loading states](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/streaming.md#route-level-loading-states-with-loadingpyxl). - **Server-side page caching with incremental regeneration.** A `@server` loader can now return a `{"data": ..., "revalidate": N}` envelope to cache its rendered HTML for `N` seconds — later requests are served from the cache without re-running the loader or the Node SSR render. A loader-less page can opt in the same way with a module-level `CACHE = {"revalidate": N}` directive. And `pyxle build --static` pre-renders every loader-less, non-dynamic page to HTML at build time and warms the cache from it on startup, so the first request after a deploy is a hit — no cold render. Once the window passes, the stale page is served instantly while a single background re-render refreshes it (incremental static regeneration — no thundering herd). Purge a route on demand from an action with `await cache.invalidate("/path")` (or `cache.invalidate_all()`). Routes already declared in the `pyxle.config.json` edge `cache` block are server-cached automatically under the same TTL. Cached responses carry a strong `ETag` (so conditional requests get a `304`) and an `x-pyxle-cache: HIT|STALE|MISS` header. The cache is enabled for `pyxle serve` (off in `pyxle dev`) and stores to a bounded in-memory backend by default, or to local disk or a shared Redis via `PYXLE_PAGE_CACHE_BACKEND` (Redis needs `pip install 'pyxle-framework[redis]'`). Only cache pages that render no per-user data — a cached render is shared with every visitor. See [Caching](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/caching.md). ## 0.4.5 - **Signed cookies & tokens — `sign_cookie` / `verify_cookie`.** New stdlib-only helpers (importable from `pyxle`) attach a tamper-proof HMAC-SHA256 signature to any string — a session id, a "remember me" token, a stateless unsubscribe or password-reset link — and verify it later in constant time. The secret comes from `PYXLE_SECRET_KEY` (or an explicit `secret_key=`), and a `salt=` namespaces signatures so a token minted for one purpose can't be replayed for another. Signing without a secret raises `MissingSecretKeyError` rather than returning an unprotected value — it fails closed. See [Security → Signed cookies and tokens](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/security.md#signed-cookies-and-tokens). - **Fix: `PYXLE_PUBLIC_*` client env vars now work in `pyxle dev` and no longer break `pyxle build`.** The generated Vite `define` emitted each value as a bare expression, so a non-identifier value (an API URL, a `0x…` Turnstile key) was silently dropped in dev and crashed `vite build` with esbuild's "Invalid define value". Values are now emitted as quoted JS string literals, so `import.meta.env.PYXLE_PUBLIC_*` resolves correctly in both dev and production builds. See [Environment variables → Client-side variables](https://pyxle.dev/docs/guides/environment-variables.md). - **Fix: web fonts and other CSS `url()` assets no longer 404 in `pyxle dev`.** Vite rewrites `@font-face`/`url(...)` references to root-relative `/@fs/…` paths, which the browser resolved against Pyxle's origin instead of Vite's and 404'd — so `@fontsource` `.woff2` files silently failed to load in development (production builds were unaffected, as their assets are hashed and served by Pyxle). Pyxle now sets Vite's `server.origin`, so dev assets load from Vite directly. ## 0.4.4 - **Fix: cross-page hash links scroll to their anchor.** Client-side navigation to `/page#section` changed the page but stayed pinned to the top (only the URL carried the hash); a full load scrolled correctly. The router now jumps to the top and then scrolls to the anchor as soon as the next page's DOM commits, matching native behaviour — including for unknown anchors, which stay at the top. ## 0.4.3 - **Security: hardened HEAD sanitisation (XSS).** Dynamic `HEAD` values/callables that interpolate loader data into raw head strings — the documented dynamic-meta-tags recipe — could previously inject markup because attribute values weren't escaped (a `"` in a `` broke out of the attribute). Head elements are now parsed and rebuilt from a strict tag allowlist with every attribute value HTML-escaped, `on*` handlers dropped, `javascript:`/`vbscript:`/`data:` URLs neutralised, `` and non-head tags rejected, and any markup injected after a breakout discarded. Inline `
